body_txt: In the hot Tuesday-night sitcom Wonderman , Bob, Charlie, and Dave are all in love with Alice, the protagonist. When the time finally comes for Alice to make her choice, she takes Bob as her one and only. What a happy ending! What a happy couple (hopefully). The audience should wholeheartedly share their joy — but they don't, because they've become emotionally invested in Charlie and Dave and wanted them to be happy. This is awkward, especially if Alice spent the entire run of the show making each man think he had a chance. The audience is supposed to support Alice and Bob, but this is difficult if the pairing has "casualties" (or competition that's still floating around). This is especially problematic if the writers had too much fun toying with the idea of Alice and Charlie earlier in the series, which can lead to a fandom rife with Shipping Wars, where half of the fandom will be unhappy no matter what happens. It's especially bad if the Alice and Bob relationship feels rushed and undercooked and/or she has more history, more time on screen, or just more sparks with Dave. So, the writers tend to whip up some contrivances to deal with this. Given the romantic resolution occurs late in the series, this has to be applied rather quickly, as there isn't much time left. This can create a subset of moderate shippers, who aren't bothered so much at being unfulfilled as it being done in a silly manner. Common variations have former competitors: Often the fate of lesbians. As a type of backlash , sympathetic fanfic will often get rid of the heterosexual competition in this fashion as well. Often the fate of the "second" father when First Father Wins . Some ways of making it so that cleaning up loose ends doesn't have to happen are for all characters to be paired up from the very beginning or reach a mutual Marry Them All solution. Or, of course, simply accepting that they lost and moving on with their life .
